notes.section_title: Introduction: The Structure of Indian Culture

topics.topic_name: Indian Culture as an Ancient Tree

content.text: Indian culture is often compared to an ancient tree with deep roots and many branches. Like a tree, Indian civilisation has nurtured a strong common trunk from which many different cultural branches have emerged. These branches represent different manifestations of Indian culture — including art, literature, science, medicine, religion, and the art of governance. However, despite their diversity, all these branches remain united by a common trunk, representing the underlying unity in Indian culture.

The roots of Indian culture are believed to extend as far back as the Indus or Harappan or Sindhu-Saraswati civilisation (3300–1300 BCE). Over millenniums, various schools of thought emerged in India, each developing its own unique personality while contributing to the broader fabric of Indian civilisation. By understanding these schools and their roots, we can better understand 'India, that is Bharat'.

Groups of thinkers or spiritual seekers who share similar ideas about human life, the world, spirituality, and the search for deeper truths. These schools differ in their approaches but are united by their quest for understanding the fundamental nature of existence.

Concerned with the spirit or soul (ātman in Sanskrit and many Indian languages). Spirituality is the search for a deeper or higher dimension beyond our current personality, involving introspection, meditation, and the pursuit of enlightenment.

The word 'Veda' comes from the Sanskrit 'vid', meaning 'knowledge'. The Vedas are the most ancient texts of India and among the most ancient texts in the world. They consist of four sacred scriptures: the Ṛig Veda, the Yajur Veda, the Sāma Veda, and the Atharva Veda.

The Vedas consist of thousands of hymns — prayers in the form of poems and songs — that were composed in the early form of Sanskrit. These hymns were recited orally and transmitted orally, not written down. They were composed in the Sapta Sindhava region (the seven river valleys we visited in Chapter 5). It is difficult to say exactly when the Ṛig Veda, the most ancient of the four, was composed; experts have proposed dates ranging from the 5th to the 2nd millennium BCE.

For over 100 to 200 generations, these texts have been passed on orally through rigorous training methods, with hardly any alterations. This meticulous transmission over thousands of years explains why, in 2008, UNESCO recognised Vedic chanting as 'a masterpiece of the oral and intangible heritage of humanity'. The method of oral transmission, called 'Vedic chanting', ensured the preservation of these sacred texts with remarkable accuracy.

content.title: UNESCO Recognition
content.text: UNESCO (United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization) is an international organisation that promotes dialogue between people and nations through education, science, and culture. It recognised the Vedic oral tradition as an exceptional example of living cultural heritage.

The Vedic hymns were composed by rishis (male seers or sages) and rishikas (female ones). They were addressed in poetical form to many deities (gods or goddesses), such as Indra, Agni, Varuṇa, Mitra, Sarasvatī, Uṣhas, and many more. Together with the seers, these deities sustained ṛitam, or truth and order in human life and in the 'cosmos' (the world or universe as an ordered and harmonious system).

The early rishis and rishikas saw those gods and goddesses not as separate, unrelated beings, but as manifestations of one supreme reality. This understanding is expressed in a famous hymn: 'ekam sat viprā bahudha vadanti' — 'The Existent [that is, the supreme reality] is one, but sages give it many names.' This concept of underlying unity in apparent multiplicity is a foundational idea in Indian thought.

From the beginning, certain values were especially important in the Vedic worldview, with 'Truth' (Ṛita) often being another name for 'God' or the ultimate reality. Truth was not merely factual accuracy but represented cosmic order and rightness. The Ṛig Veda also calls for unity among people, as expressed in this hymn: 'Come together, speak together; may your minds be of one accord ... United be your purpose, united your heart ... may your thoughts be united, so all may agree!'
A particular view or understanding of the world, its origin, or its workings. It represents the fundamental assumptions and beliefs that shape how a culture interprets reality.

Early Vedic society was organised in different janas or 'clans', that is, larger groups of people. The Ṛig Veda alone lists over 30 such janas — for instance, the Bharatas, the Purus, the Kurus, the Yadus, and the Turvaśhas. Each clan was associated with a particular region of the northwest part of the Indian subcontinent, suggesting that these were geographically organised societies.

Not much is known about how these janas governed their society. The Vedas only give us a few clues through specific words: rājā (a king or ruler), sabhā and samiti, both of which refer to a collective gathering or assembly. The existence of these terms suggests that decision-making involved some form of collective participation, though the exact nature of governance remains unclear.

Many professions are mentioned in the Vedic texts, including agriculturist, weaver, potter, builder, carpenter, healer, dancer, barber, and priest. This diversity of professions suggests a complex, specialised society where different groups of people performed different roles and contributed to the functioning of the community.
Someone who uses traditional practices to relieve or heal diseases. Healers were an important part of Vedic society, indicating that healthcare was recognised as a specialised skill.

topics.topic_name: Vedic Schools of Thought and Rituals

content.text: Vedic culture developed many rituals (yajña, often read as 'yagya') directed towards various deities (gods or goddesses) for individual or collective benefit and wellbeing. Daily rituals were generally in the form of prayers and offerings to Agni, the deity associated with fire. Over time, however, these rituals became increasingly complex.

A group of texts known as the 'Upaniṣhads' built upon Vedic concepts and introduced new ones. They developed the concept of ātman or Self — the divine essence that resides in every being but is ultimately one with brahman (the supreme reality, not to be confused with the god Brahmā). From this understanding came the famous teaching that everything — human life, nature, and the universe — is one divine essence. According to one school of thought, generally known as 'Vedanta', everything is ultimately one, called brahman or sometimes just tat ('that').

The Upaniṣhads also introduced important concepts such as rebirth (taking birth again and again in different forms) and karma (the results of one's actions). According to this worldview, one's current life circumstances are determined by the actions (karma) of previous lives, and one's present actions will shape future lives. The ultimate goal became mokṣha or liberation from this cycle of birth and death.

content.title: Key Mantras from the Upaniṣhads
content.text: Two well-known mantras express the central teaching of the Upaniṣhads in a simple but profound way: 'Aham brahma asmi' — 'I am brahman' (that is to say, I am divine). And 'Tat tvam asi' — 'You are That', meaning that the divine essence in me is the same as the divine essence in you.
The quality or state of being aware, for instance of something within oneself. In Vedantic philosophy, consciousness is the fundamental nature of brahman, the supreme reality.

topics.topic_name: Stories and Teachings from the Upaniṣhads

content.text: The Upaniṣhads teach through stories and dialogues that emphasise the importance of asking questions, whether these questions come from men, women, or children. The following stories illustrate key concepts and the teaching method of the Upaniṣhads.

content.title: Śhvetaketu and the Seed (Chhāndogya Upaniṣhad)
content.text: Rishi Uddālaka Āruṇi sent his son, Śhvetaketu, to a gurukula (traditional school) to learn the Vedas. When Śhvetaketu returned 12 years later, his father realised he had become very proud of his learning. So Uddālaka tested him with questions on the nature of brahman, which Śhvetaketu could not answer. Uddālaka then explained that brahman, though invisible, is everywhere — just as the seed of a banyan fruit seems empty when you open it, but already contains the future banyan tree. Similarly, all different pots can be made out of the same clay. Everything around us has emerged from the same essence — brahman. The father concluded with: 'Everything consists of this subtle essence. That, Śhvetaketu.' This story teaches that reality is fundamentally one, though it manifests in many diverse forms.

content.title: Nachiketa and his Quest (Katha Upaniṣhad)
content.text: Once, a man was giving away all his possessions in a ritual. As his son Nachiketa kept asking him which god he would be offered to, the father became angry and answered, 'I give you to Yama' — the god of death. Nachiketa then proceeded to Yama's world and, after a long wait, met the mighty god. His question was: 'What happens after the death of the body?' Yama tried to avoid answering, but the boy persisted. Pleased by Nachiketa's dedication, Yama explained that the ātman, or self, is hidden within all creatures. It is neither born nor does it die; it is immortal. Having acquired this profound knowledge, Nachiketa returned to his father, who welcomed him joyfully. This story emphasises the immortal nature of the self and the value of persistent spiritual inquiry.

content.title: The Debate of Gārgī (Bṛihadāraṇyaka Upaniṣhad)
content.text: Once, the wise king Janaka announced a prize for the winner of a philosophical debate. Yājñavalkya, a renowned rishi, came to the king's court and defeated many scholars until Gārgī, a rishika, asked him a series of questions on the nature of truth, the world, and finally on the nature of brahman. At that point, Yājñavalkya asked her to stop asking further questions. Later, however, Gārgī resumed her questions and Yājñavalkya went on to explain how brahman is what makes the world, the seasons, the rivers and everything else possible. This story illustrates that women were respected as seekers of truth and participated in philosophical discussions.

About two-and-a-half millenniums ago, a young prince named Siddhārtha Gautama was born in Lumbini (today in Nepal). Depending on the sources they use, scholars come up with widely different conclusions as regards the precise year of his birth. Different scholars propose dates from the 5th to the 6th century BCE. In this chapter, we choose 560 BCE as an approximate year, though the exact date matters less than understanding the significance of his teachings.

Siddhārtha Gautama grew up living a protected life in the palace. One day, at the age of 29, he asked to be driven through the city in a chariot, and for the first time in his life came across an old man, a sick man, and a dead body. He also saw an ascetic, who appeared to be happy and at peace. Following this experience, Siddhārtha decided to give up his palace life, leaving behind his wife and son. Travelling on foot as an ascetic, he met other ascetics and scholars and searched for the root cause of suffering in human life.

After meditating for many days under a pipal tree at Bodh Gaya (today in Bihar), he attained enlightenment. He realised that avidyā (ignorance) and attachment are the source of human suffering and conceived a method to remove these two causes. Siddhārtha then became known as the 'Buddha', which means the 'enlightened' or 'awakened' one.
Someone who engages in a rigorous discipline to attain a higher consciousness. Ascetics often renounce worldly pleasures and live austere lives dedicated to spiritual pursuits.
The condition of having a bond with someone or something, usually through sentiment or habit. In Buddhist philosophy, attachment to desires and possessions is seen as a primary cause of suffering.

The Buddha started teaching what he had realised, including the idea of ahimsa, which is generally translated as 'non-violence', but originally means 'non-hurting' or 'non-injuring'. This concept goes beyond physical non-violence; it includes not causing harm through words, thoughts, and intentions. He also insisted on a sincere inner discipline — not merely following rules externally, but cultivating positive values from within.

content.title: The Buddha's Teaching on Purity
content.text: The Buddha expressed his teaching with a famous saying: 'Not by water is one made pure, though many people may bathe here [in sacred rivers]. But one is pure in whom truth and dharma reside. Conquering oneself is greater than conquering a thousand men on the battlefield a thousand times.' This teaching emphasises that true purity comes from inner transformation and moral conduct, not from external rituals or victories.

Buddhism introduced a new worldview that focused on ending suffering through ethical conduct, meditation, and right understanding. The Four Noble Truths — that suffering exists, that it has a cause, that it can end, and that there is a path to end it — became central to Buddhist practice. The Eightfold Path provided practical guidelines for ethical living and spiritual development.

topics.topic_name: The Sangha and Spread of Buddhism

content.text: The Buddha founded the Sangha, a community of bhikṣhus (monks) and, later, bhikṣhuṇīs (nuns) who dedicated themselves to practicing and spreading his teachings. Both monks and nuns began travelling across the land to spread their respective teachings far and wide. Some of them created new monasteries in faraway places, while others led ascetic lives in caves cut into the rock. Archaeological findings have revealed many traces of those monasteries, sometimes even with the names of the monks who lived in the rock-cut caves and slept on stone beds.

The influence of Buddhism on India and indeed the whole of Asia was enormous. It spread along trade routes and eventually became a major religion in many Asian countries. Its impact is still perceptible today, over 2,000 years later. Stone panels from ancient monasteries, some 1,800 years old, show the Buddha teaching and provide evidence of the sophisticated artistic tradition that accompanied the spread of Buddhism.
A man who, giving up the usual life in the world, dedicates himself to religious or spiritual pursuits. A monk usually takes vows, committing himself to follow strict rules for a disciplined life.
The female equivalent of a monk. Nuns, or bhikṣhuṇīs in Buddhism, were accorded similar respect and authority as monks in the Buddhist community.

topics.topic_name: Life of Mahāvīra and Origins of Jainism

content.text: Jainism is another important school of thought that became widespread around the same time as Buddhism, although its roots are said to be much more ancient. Just like Siddhārtha Gautama, Prince Vardhamāna was born into a royal family in the early 6th century BCE. His birthplace was near the city of Vaiśhālī, in modern-day Bihar.

At the age of 30, he decided to leave his home and go in search of spiritual knowledge. He practised an ascetic discipline and, after 12 years, achieved 'infinite knowledge' or supreme wisdom. He became known as 'Mahāvīra', or 'great hero', and started preaching what he had realised. The word 'Jain' or jaina comes from jina, meaning 'conqueror'. This does not refer to the conquest of territory or enemies, but to the conquest of ignorance and attachments, so as to reach enlightenment.

An ancient Indian religion and philosophy founded by Mahāvīra in the 6th century BCE. It emphasises strict adherence to non-violence (ahimsa), non-possession (aparigraha), and the belief in many-sidedness (anekāntavāda). Jainism teaches that individuals can achieve liberation through their own efforts by following ethical practices.

Jain teachings include ahimsa (non-violence), aparigraha (non-possession), and anekāntavāda (many-sidedness). These ideas, shared to a large extent with Buddhism and the Vedantic school of thought, are central to Indian culture.

Means 'not just one' aspect or perspective. That is, the truth has many aspects and cannot be fully described by any single statement. This principle teaches humility and respect for different viewpoints, acknowledging that reality is complex and multifaceted.

Means 'non-possession' and advises detachment from material possessions, limiting oneself to what is truly necessary in life. This principle encourages simplicity and freedom from greed and excessive consumption.

content.title: Mahāvīra's Teaching on Ahimsa
content.text: The first principle of Jainism can be illustrated by this saying of Mahāvīra: 'All breathing, existing, living, sentient creatures should not be slain, nor treated with violence, nor abused, nor tormented, nor driven away.' This comprehensive statement shows that ahimsa extends to all living beings, from humans to the smallest insects.

Jainism also insists on the interconnectedness and interdependence of all creatures, from humans to invisible organisms. They support each other and cannot live without one another. Scientists studying nature, flora and fauna, have again and again confirmed this deep truth that the Jain sages understood millenniums ago.

topics.topic_name: Stories and Teachings: Jātaka Tales and Jain Stories

content.text: Both Buddhism and Jainism used stories to teach their values. The Jātaka tales, which have delighted generations of Indian children and adults, tell stories of the Buddha's former births and express Buddhist values in simple terms. Similarly, Jain stories illustrate the importance of right action and ethical living.

content.title: The Story of the Monkey-King (Buddhist Jātaka Tale)
content.text: In a well-known tale, the Buddha was the king of a large troop of monkeys. They lived near a huge tree which bore fruit of divine fragrance and taste. Despite the monkey-king's instructions that no fruit should escape, one day a ripe fruit fell into the stream. Carried by the current, it was caught in a net and taken to the palace. The king was so enchanted by its taste that he ordered his soldiers to locate the tree it came from. After a long search, they found the tree — and the monkeys enjoying the fruits. The soldiers attacked, and the only way for the monkey-king to save his monkeys was to help them cross the stream, but they could not do so on their own. Being much larger than them, the monkey-king caught hold of a tree on the other bank and let them use his body as a bridge to cross the stream, although he was severely bruised in the process and eventually died. The king, who watched the scene from a distance, was greatly moved by the monkey-king's selfless sacrifice and thought about the role of a king with respect to his subjects. This story teaches sacrifice, compassion, and the duty of a leader to protect those under his care.

content.title: A Jain Story: Rohineya the Burglar
content.text: Rohineya was an extraordinarily skilled burglar who evaded all attempts to catch him. On his way to a city, he accidentally heard a few sentences from a sermon that Mahāvīra was giving about achieving liberation from the ordinary life of ignorance. Reaching the city, Rohineya was recognised and arrested. A minister devised a clever plan to force him to confess his identity. But Rohineya, remembering Mahāvīra's words, was able to detect the minister's plan and defeat it. Feeling remorseful, Rohineya approached Mahāvīra, confessed his crimes, returned the stolen treasures, and asked for forgiveness. He became a monk, realised the illusion he was living in, and focused on acquiring higher knowledge. The story illustrates the importance of right action and right thinking, and also illustrates the fact that everyone should have a second chance.

Both Buddhist and Jain monks travelled across India creating monasteries and places of worship. Some of these were built in accessible areas, while others were cut into rock faces in remote locations. The most famous examples of rock-cut architecture from between the 6th and 10th centuries CE are found at Ellora in Maharashtra, where caves dedicated to Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain traditions stand side by side, indicating the peaceful coexistence of different religious traditions.

Archaeological findings from these cave monasteries have revealed remarkable evidence of monastic life — stone beds where monks slept, places for meditation, and artistic depictions of the Buddha teaching and religious scenes. These discoveries provide tangible evidence of how Buddhism and Jainism spread and developed as living traditions across ancient India.

Although the Vedic, Buddhist, and Jain schools had important differences in their methods and philosophies, they also shared some common concepts. All three were concerned with dharma (righteous living), karma (the law of action and consequence), rebirth (the cycle of birth and death), and the search for an end to suffering and ignorance. These shared concerns represent the 'trunk' of the tree of Indian culture, uniting diverse branches.

rows: Based on the Vedas as ultimate source

content.text: Despite these differences, all three schools were fundamentally concerned with understanding the cause of suffering and finding a means of removing ignorance and ending the cycle of suffering. They all emphasised ethical living, spiritual discipline, and the pursuit of a higher truth. The coexistence and mutual influence of these schools demonstrates the pluralistic nature of Indian civilisation.

While the Vedic, Buddhist, and Jain schools of thought are well documented in texts, India has also had rich 'oral traditions' — that is, teachings or practices transmitted through everyday practice, without written texts. Among these are numerous folk traditions (transmitted by common people) and tribal traditions (transmitted by tribes). These oral traditions have been central to Indian culture and have preserved knowledge, values, and practices across generations.

Folk traditions include stories, songs, festivals, rituals, and art forms that are passed down within communities. These traditions embody the wisdom, values, and worldviews of ordinary people and have a direct connection to daily life, seasons, and the natural environment. Tribal traditions similarly preserve the knowledge and practices specific to tribal communities, often reflecting a deep understanding of the local environment and sustainable living practices.

topics.topic_name: Definition and Numbers of Tribes in India

content.text: Today, anthropologists usually consider a tribe to be a group of families or clans sharing a tradition of common descent, a common culture and a language, living as a close-knit community under a chief and holding no private property. The Constitution of India uses the terms 'tribes' and 'tribal communities' in English and 'janjāti' in Hindi.

content.title: Historical Note
content.text: Interestingly, ancient India did not have a specific word for 'tribe' as tribes were just different janas that lived in a specific environment, such as forests or mountains. The concept of 'tribe' as a category is partly a modern administrative construct.

According to official figures, in 2011 India had 705 tribes spread over most States, amounting to a population of about 104 million people — more than the populations of Australia and the United Kingdom together. This significant population represents the rich diversity of India's tribal heritage. In the 19th century, anthropologists often described tribes as 'primitive' or 'inferior' to civilised people. However, with deeper studies of tribal communities and their rich and complex cultures, such biased judgments have been mostly abandoned.

topics.topic_name: Interaction Between Folk, Tribal, and Great Traditions

content.text: There has been a constant interaction between folk and tribal traditions, and the leading schools of thought such as Buddhism, Jainism, and Hinduism. Deities, concepts, legends, and rituals have been freely exchanged in both directions. For instance, according to one tradition, Jagannath, worshipped at Puri (Odisha), was originally a tribal deity; this is also the case with various forms of the mother-goddess worshipped across India. Some tribes, on the other hand, have adopted Hindu deities long ago, and possess their own versions of the Mahābhārata and the Rāmāyaṇa — this has been well documented from India's northeastern States, all the way to Tamil Nadu.

How have such interactions taken place for so long and so naturally? It is, in the end, because folk, tribal, and Hindu belief systems have many similar concepts. For instance, in all three, elements of nature such as mountains, rivers, trees, plants, and animals, and some stones too, are regarded as sacred, because there is consciousness behind all of them. Indeed, tribes generally worship many deities associated with those natural elements.

content.title: Sacred Mountains of the Todas
content.text: The Toda tribals of the Nilgiris of Tamil Nadu, for instance, worship over thirty peaks of this mountain range as residences of a god or a goddess; those peaks are so sacred that the Todas avoid pointing to them with a finger. This reverence for natural features as sacred spaces is shared across many tribal and Hindu traditions.

topics.topic_name: Concept of Higher Divinity in Tribal Traditions

content.text: Despite the multiplicity of deities and sacred entities, many tribal groups have a concept of a higher divinity or supreme being. For example, several tribes of Arunachal Pradesh worship Donyipolo, a combined form of the Sun and the Moon who later rose to the higher status of a supreme god. This is also the case of the god Khandoba in parts of central India. In eastern India, the Munda and Santhal tribals, among others, worship Singbonga, a supreme deity who created this whole world. There are many more such examples.

This demonstrates that tribal belief systems, while focusing on the sacred aspects of nature and local deities, often also recognise and respect a concept of ultimate divinity. This parallel with the monotheistic and monistic tendencies in Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain thought shows the underlying philosophical sophistication of tribal traditions.

The Indian sociologist André Béteille summed up the situation in these words: 'The thousands of castes and tribes on the Indian subcontinent have influenced each other in their religious beliefs and practices since the beginning of history and before. That the tribal religions have been influenced by Hinduism is widely accepted, but it is equally true that Hinduism, not only in its formative phase but throughout its evolution, has been influenced by tribal religions.' This statement captures the essence of cultural synthesis in India.

The result of this long interaction has been mutual enrichment. In this manner, folk and tribal beliefs and practices also count among India's cultural roots. Tribal belief systems generally regard the land and its features as sacred; they often have, at the same time, a higher concept of divinity. There has been free borrowing and giving from every side, resulting in a rich, diverse, and dynamic cultural landscape.

content.title: Why 'Schools of Thought' Instead of 'Religions'?
content.text: In English, Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism, and Sikhism are often labelled 'religions'. However, many scholars prefer to use the terms 'schools of thought' and 'belief systems' when discussing Indian traditions. This is because there are multiple aspects to those schools and systems: a philosophical aspect, a spiritual aspect, a religious aspect, an ethical aspect, a social aspect, and others. Many scholars agree that the word 'religion' is too limiting in the context of the Indian civilisation, where spirituality, philosophy, ethics, and social organisation are deeply intertwined.

Each school of thought in India contains not just religious beliefs but also philosophical frameworks for understanding reality, ethical codes for living, social structures for organisation, and spiritual practices for inner development. This holistic nature of Indian traditions makes the term 'religion' insufficient, as it typically refers only to the belief and worship aspect.

topics.topic_name: Materialist Schools and Diversity of Ideas

content.text: There were yet other schools of thought in ancient India. For example, one of them, known as the 'Chārvāka' school (sometimes also 'Lokāyata'), believed that this material world is the only thing that exists, and therefore there can be no life after death. This school does not seem to have gained much popularity and it disappeared with time. We mention it to show that there was a wide diversity of intellectual or spiritual belief systems; people were free to choose what suited them.

This existence of dissenting views — materialist philosophies that rejected the concepts of rebirth and an ultimate spiritual reality — demonstrates that Indian intellectual tradition was not monolithic. There was space for debate, disagreement, and the coexistence of contradictory worldviews. This openness to diverse perspectives is itself a characteristic feature of Indian culture.

glossary.definition: Non-violence or more precisely, non-hurting and non-injuring. It extends beyond physical violence to include non-violence in thought, speech, and intention. Central to Buddhist and Jain philosophy.
The individual soul or self in Hindu and Indian philosophical traditions. In Vedantic philosophy, the ātman is ultimately identical with brahman, the ultimate reality.

A Jain principle meaning 'not just one' perspective. It teaches that truth is multifaceted and cannot be fully described by any single statement, encouraging humility and respect for diverse viewpoints.
Non-possession or non-attachment. A Jain principle advising detachment from material possessions and limiting oneself to what is truly necessary in life.
A person who engages in rigorous spiritual discipline, often renouncing worldly pleasures to pursue higher consciousness and spiritual goals.
The condition of having emotional or material bonds with people or things. In Buddhist philosophy, attachment to desires and possessions is a primary cause of suffering.
A Buddhist monk who has taken vows to follow monastic discipline and dedicates himself to spiritual practice and teaching.
A Buddhist nun; the female equivalent of a bhikṣhu. Nuns in Buddhism are accorded similar respect and authority as monks.
The ultimate, supreme reality in Hindu and Vedantic philosophy; often described as non-dual consciousness. Not to be confused with Brahmā, the creator god.
Means 'the enlightened' or 'awakened' one. Refers to Siddhārtha Gautama, who attained enlightenment and founded Buddhism.
An ancient Indian materialist school of thought that believed only the material world exists and rejected concepts of afterlife and spirituality.
The quality or state of being aware, particularly of something within oneself. In Vedantic philosophy, consciousness is the fundamental nature of brahman.
The world or universe viewed as an ordered and harmonious system. In Vedic thought, the cosmos is seen as an interconnected whole governed by dharma (cosmic order).
Righteousness, virtue, and duty; also refers to cosmic order and law. It is a fundamental concept across Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain traditions.
In Buddhist and Jain philosophy, the state of awakening to ultimate truth and freedom from ignorance and suffering. Also called nirvāṇa in Buddhism and mokṣha in Jainism.
A spiritual teacher or master. In Indian tradition, a guru is respected as a guide who leads students towards spiritual knowledge and enlightenment.
A traditional Indian school or learning community, typically based in the guru's household, where students lived and studied the Vedas and various disciplines under the guidance of a master.
A person who uses traditional practices and knowledge to relieve or cure diseases. Healers were an important part of ancient Indian society.
A follower of Jainism; also, the adjective referring to Jain tradition, practices, or philosophy.

An ancient Indian religion founded by Mahāvīra in the 6th century BCE, emphasising ahimsa (non-violence), aparigraha (non-possession), and individual liberation through strict ethical and ascetic practices.
A clan or tribe in ancient Vedic society. The Ṛig Veda mentions over 30 janas, each associated with a particular geographical region.
Buddhist stories of the Buddha's previous lives. The Jātaka tales are used to teach Buddhist values and moral lessons in engaging narrative form.
Knowledge, particularly spiritual or experiential knowledge of the ultimate reality. In Vedantic philosophy, jnana (knowledge of Brahman) is the path to liberation.

The law of action and consequence; the idea that one's actions (in this life and previous lives) determine one's future circumstances and experiences. A central concept in Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain thought.
A deity worshipped in parts of central India, illustrating the blend of tribal and Hindu religious traditions.
An alternative name for the Chārvāka materialist school of ancient Indian philosophy.
One of the two major Sanskrit epics of ancient India, containing the story of a dynastic conflict and much philosophical and ethical teaching, including the Bhagavad Gita.
Means 'great hero'. The title given to Vardhamāna, the founder of Jainism in the 6th century BCE, who achieved enlightenment and taught the path of strict non-violence and asceticism.
A verse or formula, often from sacred texts like the Vedas and Upaniṣhads, used for meditation, chanting, or spiritual teaching. Mantras are believed to have spiritual power.
Liberation or release from the cycle of birth and death (samsāra). The ultimate goal in Hindu, Jain, and some Buddhist philosophies.
A man who renounces worldly life and dedicates himself to religious or spiritual pursuits, typically living in a monastery and following strict disciplinary rules and vows.
The philosophical view that all reality is ultimately one. In Indian philosophy, the Vedantic concept that everything is manifestations of the single reality, Brahman.
Belief in a single, supreme God. Some traditions, particularly in tribal religions, recognise a supreme deity while also honouring many other sacred powers and deities.
In Buddhism, the state of enlightenment and release from suffering and the cycle of rebirth. Often described as the extinction of ignorance and craving.
Not divided into two; referring to the philosophical view that reality is ultimately one undivided whole. The Vedantic concept of Brahman is non-dual.
A woman who renounces worldly life and dedicates herself to religious or spiritual pursuits. In Buddhism, nuns (bhikṣhuṇīs) hold an important place in the monastic community.
Knowledge, teachings, or practices transmitted verbally from generation to generation without being written down. Central to Indian culture, including Vedic recitation and folk traditions.
A sacred tree (Ficus religiosa) in Indian tradition. The Buddha is said to have attained enlightenment while meditating under a pipal tree at Bodh Gaya.
One of the two major Sanskrit epics of ancient India, telling the story of Rāma and containing important ethical and spiritual teachings.
The cyclical process of being born again in different forms according to one's karma. A central concept in Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain philosophies, contrasting with the idea of a single linear life.
A male seer, sage, or spiritual master in Indian tradition. Rishis are credited with receiving and composing the Vedic hymns.
A female seer, sage, or spiritual master. Rishikas are recognised in Indian tradition as equally capable of spiritual knowledge as their male counterparts.
Cosmic order, truth, and righteousness in Vedic philosophy. Often another name for the ultimate reality and the principle governing the universe.
The oldest and most sacred of the four Vedas, consisting of hymns composed in Sanskrit and transmitted orally for thousands of years. It is among the oldest known texts in the world.
Holy or worthy of religious respect and veneration. In Indian tradition, sacred elements include natural features (mountains, rivers), texts (Vedas), and deities.
A collective assembly or council in ancient Vedic society; appears to have served a role in community decision-making and governance.
The community of monks and nuns in Buddhism. The Sangha is one of the Three Jewels of Buddhism, alongside the Buddha and the Dharma.
An ancient Indo-European language in which the Vedas and many classical Indian texts were composed. Still used in Hindu rituals and considered a sacred language in Indian tradition.
Groups of thinkers or spiritual seekers who share similar ideas and philosophies about the nature of reality, ethics, and spirituality. Major Indian schools include Vedanta, Buddhism, and Jainism.
Someone who actively searches for spiritual truth and wisdom. In Indian tradition, seekers may be sages, saints, yogis, or philosophers dedicated to understanding ultimate reality.
Capable of feeling, perceiving, or experiencing. In Jain philosophy, sentience extends to all living beings, even microscopic organisms, deserving of ethical consideration.
The birth name of the Buddha. A prince who renounced his palace life, sought spiritual truth, attained enlightenment, and founded Buddhism around the 6th century BCE.
A monotheistic religion founded in Punjab in the 15th century CE by Guru Nanak, combining Hindu and Islamic influences with its own unique philosophy and practices.
A supreme deity worshipped by the Munda and Santhal tribal communities in eastern India, illustrating tribal concepts of ultimate divinity.

The search for a deeper or higher dimension of existence beyond one's ordinary personality. Spirituality in Indian tradition involves inner transformation, meditation, and the pursuit of enlightenment.
Sanskrit for 'That Thou Art' or 'You are That'. A famous Upaniṣhadic teaching expressing the identity of the individual self (ātman) with the ultimate reality (Brahman).

In anthropological terms, a social group characterised by common descent, shared culture and language, a cohesive community structure under a chief, and traditionally, collective rather than private property ownership.
Cultural practices, beliefs, and knowledge passed down within tribal communities, often deeply connected to the natural environment and reflecting sustainable living practices.
United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization. An international body that works to promote dialogue between cultures and preserve cultural heritage.
A philosophical text that built upon the Vedas and introduced new concepts such as ātman, brahman, karma, and rebirth. The Upaniṣhads form the foundation of Vedantic philosophy.
A school of Hindu philosophy based on the Upaniṣhads, teaching that ultimate reality (Brahman) is non-dual and that the individual self (ātman) is identical with Brahman.
The oldest and most sacred texts of Indian civilisation, consisting of hymns, prayers, and philosophical teachings composed in Sanskrit and transmitted orally for millenniums.
The civilisation and way of life of the early Indo-Aryans who composed and transmitted the Vedas, characterised by pastoral and agricultural communities organised in clans.
A particular way of understanding and interpreting the world, its origin, nature, and functioning. A worldview encompasses one's fundamental assumptions about reality and existence.
A ritual or sacrifice (also spelled yagya) in Vedic religion, typically involving offerings to deities for the benefit and wellbeing of the individual or community.
The god of death in Hindu and Indian mythology. In the Katha Upaniṣhad, Yama is depicted as a wise teacher who reveals the nature of the eternal ātman.

A school of ancient Indian philosophy focused on systematic practices and disciplines intended to achieve realisation of Brahman in one's consciousness. Also refers to physical and mental practices for spiritual development.

summary_points: Indian culture can be understood as an ancient tree with deep roots and many branches, united by a common trunk. The roots extend to the Indus/Harappan civilisation, and the branches represent diverse cultural traditions — art, science, philosophy, religion, and governance.

The Vedas are the oldest known texts of India, composed in Sanskrit and transmitted orally for over 100 generations with remarkable accuracy. They contain hymns addressed to various deities but express an underlying belief in one ultimate reality.

The Vedas introduced foundational concepts such as Ṛita (cosmic order and truth), ātman (individual self), and the idea of interconnected reality. Vedic society was organised in janas (clans), and various professions are mentioned, suggesting a complex, specialised society.

The Upaniṣhads, built upon Vedic concepts, introduced new ideas: ātman (the individual soul) is identical with Brahman (ultimate reality), and individuals experience rebirth according to their karma. The goal is mokṣha (liberation from the cycle of birth and death).

Buddhism, founded by Siddhārtha Gautama (the Buddha) in the 6th century BCE, identified suffering and ignorance as fundamental problems and proposed the Eightfold Path as a solution. It emphasises ahimsa (non-violence) and established the Sangha (monastic community) to spread its teachings.

Jainism, founded by Mahāvīra in the 6th century BCE, teaches strict adherence to ahimsa (non-violence), aparigraha (non-possession), and anekāntavāda (many-sidedness). It emphasises individual liberation through ascetic discipline and ethical conduct.

All major Indian schools of thought — Vedic, Buddhist, Jain — share common concepts such as karma (law of action and consequence), rebirth, dharma (righteousness and duty), and the goal of liberation. These concepts form the trunk uniting diverse branches of Indian culture.

Indian culture is not limited to the 'great traditions' of Vedic, Buddhist, and Jain thought. Folk traditions and tribal traditions have been equally important and have engaged in constant interaction with the major philosophical schools, resulting in mutual enrichment and synthesis.

Tribal belief systems recognise the sacred nature of natural elements (mountains, rivers, animals) and often acknowledge a concept of ultimate divinity. Examples include Singbonga (of the Munda and Santhal), Donyipolo (of Arunachal tribes), and Khandoba (central India).

Indian culture is characterised by pluralism and the peaceful coexistence of diverse belief systems. There is philosophical space for dissenting views (like the materialist Chārvāka school), and there has been continuous, mutually beneficial interaction between folk, tribal, and philosophical traditions, creating a uniquely rich and complex civilisation.
